records.ety: [F. dfiler; pref. d-, for des- (L. dis-) + file a row or line. See File a row.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Defiled (d*fīld"); p. pr. & vb. n. Defiling.]

defs: To march off in a line, file by file; to file off.

records.ety: [Cf. F. dfil, fr. dfiler to defile.]

defs: Any narrow passage or gorge in which troops can march only in a file, or with a narrow front; a long, narrow pass between hills, rocks, etc.
The act of defilading a fortress, or of raising the exterior works in order to protect the interior. See Defilade.

records.ety: [OE. defoulen, -foilen, to tread down, OF. defouler; de- + fouler to trample (see Full, v. t.), and OE. defoulen to foul (influenced in form by the older verb defoilen). See File to defile, Foul, Defoul.]

defs: To make foul or impure; to make filthy; to dirty; to befoul; to pollute.
quotes: They that touch pitch will be defiled.

defs: To soil or sully; to tarnish, as reputation; to taint.
quotes: He is . . . among the greatest prelates of this age, however his character may be defiled by . . . dirty hands.

defs: To injure in purity of character; to corrupt.
quotes: Defile not yourselves with the idols of Egypt.

defs: To corrupt the chastity of; to debauch; to violate; to rape.
quotes: The husband murder'd and the wife defiled.

defs: To make ceremonially unclean; to pollute.
quotes: That which dieth of itself, or is torn with beasts, he shall not eat to defile therewith.
